justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1,4,6 as invariant:true; BR-2,3,5 as invariant:false). Each rule has detailed written justification clearly distinguishing state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. Rules 2,3,5 explicitly use 'configurable'/'policy knob'/'tunable parameter' reasoning. Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in both escalations and edge cases (what happens during/after the 14-day window, retry behavior during grace period). Recommendation to treat configurable values as policy knobs is implicit throughout the justifications.

observations.text: All 6 classifications correct with no errors
Justifications are exceptionally thorough — each explains WHY the classification holds, not just what it is

Clear configurable vs. invariant distinction applied systematically: BR-3 even notes the meta-point that 'some finite number of failures triggers cancellation' is closer to invariant while the specific threshold of 3 is policy
Grace period ambiguity surfaced in multiple places: escalations, edge cases, and low-confidence areas
Goes beyond minimum requirements with rich edge cases (race condition on payment validation, tier downgrade mid-grace-period, grace period overlapping next billing cycle)
Ubiquitous language section and domain analysis add significant value beyond what was asked
Second escalation about trial concurrency vs. lifetime limit surfaces an additional ambiguity not in the rubric but clearly valuable
